---
title: Get started
---

This page shows how to install `heormodel` and run a first probabilistic cost-effectiveness analysis on a results table you already have, without writing any model code. It requires Python 3.11 or newer. For the same analysis worked through in full, continue to the [bring your own outputs tutorial](tutorials/byo-outputs.qmd).

## Installing the package

With [uv](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/):

```bash
uv add heormodel
```

Or with pip:

```bash
pip install heormodel
```

The `calibration` extra adds Bayesian calibration via `pyabc`: `uv add "heormodel[calibration]"`.

## Analyzing a results table you already have

`heormodel` does not require a model engine. Any costs-and-effects table with `strategy`, `iteration`, `cost`, and `qaly` columns, whether it came from a spreadsheet export or a legacy simulator, enters the analysis through `as_outcomes`. The code below builds such a table from three synthetic strategies to stand in for that external source:

```{python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from heormodel.run import as_outcomes
from heormodel.cea import ceac, icer_table
from heormodel.voi import evpi

rng = np.random.default_rng(20260704)
n = 2_000
df = pd.concat(
    pd.DataFrame({
        "strategy": name, "iteration": range(n),
        "cost": rng.normal(cost, 1_500, n), "qaly": rng.normal(q, 0.35, n),
    })
    for name, cost, q in [("A", 30_000, 7.0), ("B", 38_000, 7.4), ("C", 55_000, 7.5)]
)

outcomes = as_outcomes(df)  # also reads a CSV path directly
icer_table(outcomes).round(1)
```

`icer_table` orders strategies by cost and reports incremental cost-effectiveness ratios along the efficiency frontier. The `status` column marks dominated (`D`) and extended-dominated (`ED`) strategies; here all three are non-dominated (`ND`), so the right choice depends on how much the decision maker is willing to pay per quality-adjusted life-year (QALY). `ceac` answers that directly: for each threshold on a grid, it reports the share of iterations in which each strategy has the highest net benefit.

```{python}
curves = ceac(outcomes, np.linspace(0, 100_000, 41))
curves.iloc[::10].round(3)
```

The expected value of perfect information (EVPI) turns that remaining uncertainty into a single number, at a chosen threshold: how much a decision maker should be willing to pay to resolve it entirely.

```{python}
print(f"EVPI at WTP 50,000: {evpi(outcomes, wtp=50_000):,.1f}")
```

Column names are remappable via keyword arguments if a table does not already use `strategy`, `iteration`, `cost`, and `qaly`; see [`as_outcomes`](reference/as_outcomes.qmd).

## Next steps

- [Bring your own outputs](tutorials/byo-outputs.qmd): the same analysis, expanded to plots, a value-of-information ranking, and a run report.
- [Markov cohort model](tutorials/mdm-cohort.qmd): the standard state-transition model, run through the same analysis. The [microsimulation engine](tutorials/microsim.qmd) is the step up in detail after it.
- [Engines](concepts/engines.qmd): the contract every engine satisfies.
